Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - GENERATIVE POSTERIOR NETWORKS FOR APPROXI - MATELY BAYESIAN EPISTEMIC UNCERTAINTY ESTI MATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Ensembles of neural networks are often used to estimate epistemic uncertainty in high-dimensional problems because of their scalability and ease of use. These methods, however, are expensive to sample from as each sample requires a new neural network to be trained from scratch. We propose a new method, Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a generative model that, given a prior distribution over functions, approximates the posterior distribution directly by regularizing the network towards samples from the prior. This allows our method to quickly sample from the posterior and construct confidence bounds. We prove theoretically that our method indeed approximates the Bayesian posterior and show empirically that it improves epistemic uncertainty estimation over competing methods.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep learning systems are becoming ubiquitous in many industries, specifically in applications with large datasets and where prediction mistakes are inexpensive. However, for many other industries, such as autonomous manufacturing, where datasets are smaller and mistakes can be catastrophic, the adoption of deep learning techniques has been slow. One of the critical challenges to applying deep learning models in these safety-critical domains is the lack of uncertainty estimation. If a model makes a prediction in a safety critical environment, we would like that model to be highly confident that prediction is correct. Uncertainty prediction can also provide significant benefit in data-poor domains by guiding exploration towards areas of uncertainty. Most deep learning models, however, are not able to estimate epistemic uncertainty – uncertainty deriving from lack of data samples. In this work, we introduce a new method for modeling epistemic uncertainty in neural networks.

We formulate this problem as a Bayesian Inference problem: given a prior distribution over functions and a set of training data, we want to model the posterior function distribution. There are many existing approaches to constructing such a posterior, such as Gaussian Processes (GPs) or Variational Inference (VI) methods, but these methods can require significant engineering to work in practice and can be expensive in high-dimensional settings. For this reason, many practitioners turn to using

ensembles of neural networks to approximate epistemic uncertainty for their ease of use in highdimensional settings. Pearce et al. (2020) illustrated that, given the correct regularization, these ensemble members approximate samples from the posterior distribution of functions. However, using this technique, every new sample of the posterior requires training a new neural network from scratch, which can be prohibitively expensive for large problems.

To address this challenge, we introduce Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a generative neural network model that directly approximates the posterior distribution by regularizing the output of the network towards samples from the prior distribution. By learning a low-dimensional latent representation of the posterior, our method can quickly sample from the posterior and construct confidence intervals. We prove that our method approximates the Bayesian posterior over functions and show empirically that our method can improves uncertainty estimation over competing methods.

## 2 RELATED WORK

There are two categories of uncertainty in the context of probabilistic modeling: aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty. Aleatoric uncertainty refers to uncertainty inherent in a system, even when the true parameters of the system are known, while epistemic uncertainty refers to modeling uncertainty that can be reduced through collecting more data (Der Kiureghian & Ditlevsen, 2009; Gal, 2016). Deep learning models have shown impressive performance in quantifying aleatoric uncertainty when given enough data. On the other hand, despite many methods seeking to estimate epistemic uncertainty due to its well-motivated applications in Bayesian Optimization (Snoek et al., 2012; Springenberg et al., 2016), Reinforcement Learning (Curi* et al., 2020), and adversarial robustness (Stutz et al., 2020), quantifying it remains a challenging problem for deep learning models.

Gaussian Processes (GPs) remain one of the most effective methods for epistemic uncertainty estimation in low-dimensional, low-data settings. While scalability can be improved with interpolation methods (Hensman et al., 2013; Titsias, 2009; Wilson & Nickisch, 2015) and kernel learning methods (Wilson et al., 2016), GPs tend to perform worse than neural network-based methods on high-dimensional problems.

Another way to formulate epistemic uncertainty estimation is as a Bayesian Inference problem. Specifically, given a prior distribution over functions and some labeled data, the goal is to construct a posterior distribution in the Bayesian sense. Because this posterior distribution is usually intractable to compute exactly, approximate sampling methods are used instead. Markov-Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) (Tanner & Wong, 1987) and Variational Inference (VI) (Blei et al., 2017) are some of the most common methods for posterior sampling. These methods, however, tend to struggle in high dimensional problems. The computational complexity of MCMC scales poorly with the dimension of the data and VI methods tend to require restrictive function families with strong independence assumptions. Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) (Blundell et al., 2015) are another way to perform Bayesian Inference a neural networks by modeling each parameter in the network with an independent Gaussian distribution. While BNNs perform well in low dimensional settings, their performance tends to degrade in high-dimensional settings that require large networks.

Gal & Ghahramani (2016) proved that dropout can also be used as a form of Bayesian Inference. Specifically, if a network is trained using dropout and L2 regularization, posterior samples can be collected by applying dropout at test-time. This method has surprisingly good out-of-distribution detection and can be applied to any network. However, it can be hard to tune the dropout parameter to have good uncertainty prediction without significantly degrading performance.

Neural network ensembling is another commonly used epistemic uncertainty estimation technique, which is closely related to our proposed method in this work. This technique involves training a number of completely separate neural networks on the same data to form an ensemble. Because of the stochasticity of initialization and the training process, each member of the ensemble should be a slightly different function. Specifically, the ensemble members should produce similar outputs in data-dense regions of the state-space and dissimilar outputs in data-sparse regions. Pearce et al. (2020) proved that, by using the correct regularization, each ensemble member becomes an approximate sample from the function posterior. Ensembling methods are particularly attractive to practitioners because they require very little fine-tuning and work surprisingly well in practice. However, these methods have one main drawback, namely that each new sample from the posterior

## 3.1 RANDOMIZED MAP SAMPLING (RMS)

Since the values _**µ**_ like and **Σ** like are intractible to compute in practice, the challenge of computing the posterior distribution remains. However, Pearce et al. (2020) showed how we can use a method called Randomized MAP sampling (RMS) to approximately sample from the posterior distribution. With RMS we first assume access to a mechanism to compute the maximum a posteriori (MAP) solution for our posterior. Then, by randomly shifting the prior distribution we use in our MAP solver, we can estimate samples from the posterior.

Specifically, RMS samples “anchor points” _**θ**_ anc from some distribution _**θ**_ anc _∼N_ ( _**µ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** anc). Each anchor point defines a shifted prior distribution _P_ anc( _**θ**_ anc) = _N_ ( _**θ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** prior). Now we define the MAP function which finds the MAP solution using this shifted prior:

By sampling different anchor points, we can then construct a probability distribution over MAP solutions _P_ ( _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc)), which can be shown to be Gaussian. Using moment matching, Pearce et al. (2020) proved, in Theorem 1, that if we choose _**µ**_ anc = _**µ**_ prior and **Σ** anc = **Σ** prior + **Σ** prior **Σ** _[−]_ like[1] **[Σ]**[prior][,] then _P_ ( _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc)) = _P_ post( _**θ** |_ **y** obs). Using the approximation **Σ** anc _≈_ **Σ** prior, a sample from posterior, _**θ**_ post, can be collected by sampling _**θ**_ anc _∼N_ ( _**µ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** anc) and optimizing the following:

## 4 GENERATIVE POSTERIOR NETWORKS

While RMS can be used to construct samples from the posterior, every new sample requires solving a new optimization problem. Instead, we propose learning the MAP function itself.

Let _g_ be a neural network parameterized by some vector, _φ_ , that takes as input a sample from the anchor distribution, _**θ**_ anc _∼ P_ anc( _**θ**_ anc), and outputs _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc). If we assume that _g_ has enough expressive power to represent the MAP function, then _g_ ( _**θ**_ anc; _φ_ ) = _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc) if:

In practice, however, this is a very hard optimization problem for two main reasons. (1) Optimizing in parameter space tends to be challenging for gradient-descent optimization. (2) The space of all parameters _**θ**_ anc is not only very large, but there are also many parameters _**θ**_ anc that map to the same outputs for all inputs. Moreover, we would expect the posterior parameters to be highly correlated and, therefore, need less expressive power to be represented.

To address these two problems, we will first change the problem definition slightly to find the posterior in _output_ space as opposed to _parameter_ space. We prove in the next section that, by changing our loss function, we can still use RMS to approximate the posterior in output space. Next, we show practically how we construct a low-dimensional representation of the anchor distribution.

## 4.3 LOW-DIMENSIONAL EMBEDDING

As mentioned above, optimizing the generative function _g_ over the the space of all anchor parameters _**θ**_ anc did not yield great results because the space was too large. Instead, we sought to learn a lowdimensional representation of the anchor parameters. There are two key reasons why we would expect to be able to decrease the representational power of the anchor distribution and maintain the same of fidelity in the posterior estimation. (1) Because of the nature of neural networks, there are many settings of parameters _**θ**_ that would result in the same output vector **Y[ˆ]** . Because we are focusing on the posterior of **Y[ˆ]** , we need less representational power to model **Y[ˆ]** . (2) And, maybe more importantly, because the posterior parameters are highly correlated, we would expect to need less expressive power to represent the posterior distribution than the prior distribution.

Thus, we would like to construct a simpler estimate of the prior space using a low-dimensional embedding vector, **z** . That is, we want our generative model to take as input the embedding vector **z** _∼N_ (0 _, I_ ), instead of _**θ**_ anc, in order to estimate the MAP function. To do this, we need to learn a mapping from anchor parameters _**θ**_ anc to embedding vectors **z** . For our experiments, we used a 1-1 embedding scheme where we sample _k_ parameters from the true prior _**θ**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**θ** k ∼ P_ prior( _**θ**_ ) and _k_ independent samples from our embedding **z** 1 _, . . . ,_ **z** _k_ . We then jointly optimize over _**φ**_ and **z** 1 _, . . . ,_ **z** _k_ as follows:

## 4.4 CLASSIFICATION

Our method uses the assumption that the prior over **Y[ˆ]** is approximately normally distributed. While this approximation is exact, assuming the prior parameters are normally distributed, only when either the network is linear or the final layer is infinitely wide and does not have an activation function, for most deep networks, this is a good approximation. However, if we add a soft-max to the final output, as is usually done for classification tasks, this assumption is violated. To get around this, for classification tasks, we add the anchor loss to the pre-softmax outputs.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

The goal of our experiments is to illustrate the ability of our method to accurately model epistemic uncertainty for out of distribution data while retaining high performance on in distribution data. We will test our method on three problems: (1) a small-scale regression task, (2) Bayesian Optimization (BO), and (3) Classification with out of distribution examples.

We compare our method against 3 competing Bayesian methods: Gaussian Processes (GPs), Bayesian Dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016), and anchor-regularized neural-network ensembles (Pearce et al., 2020) with 10 ensemble members. For our BO experiments we use exact GP with a static kernel since the problems are sufficiently low-dimensional, but for our classification experiments we need to use approximate GPs. Specifically, we use a grid-interpolated GP with Deep Kernel Learning (Wilson et al., 2016) implemented with the GPyTorch library (Gardner et al., 2018). For the anchor-regularized ensembles, since every member requires a unique set of parameters (and anchor points) the number of ensemble members was limited by the memory capacity of our GPUs.

## 5.1 SMALL SCALE REGRESSION

Our first experiment is a small 1-dimensional regression problem where we can easily compute the ground truth. We provide each method with the same 6 observations. For this experiment, we compute 100 samples from the approximate posterior for each method (except for the Ensemble where we only use 10 samples). For ground truth, we use the Metropolis-Hastings MCMC algorithm. Figure 2 shows the results.

## 5.2 BAYESIAN OPTIMIZATION

The goal of Bayesian Optimization is to find the maximum of a function in as few samples as possible. This is commonly done by first constructing a posterior over possible functions given the samples we have collected so far, then, through various methods, choosing sample points that might maximize the true function. By using different methods for predicting the posterior in BO, we can evaluate how well these methods capture epistemic uncertainty.

For all methods we use Thompson Sampling and implemented the experiments using the BOTorch library by Balandat et al. (2020). We tested each method on five standard benchmark BO functions: the Branin, Hartmann-6D, Shekel, Michalewicz-10D, and Powell functions. We normalize the output of each function between a minimum value (found by random sampling) and the optimal value. To make sure that each method had a large enough sample variance, we chose hyper-parameters such that 100 prior samples from each method have a range of roughly [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1]. To choose the best regularization hyper-parameter, we ran each method with 5 different regularization parameters, then chose the hyper-parameters with the highest average performance across all BO problems.

Figure 3 shows the best normalized function value sampled so far during optimization averaged across the 5 BO problems each ran for 5 trials. Our method significantly out-performs Random sampling and even slightly outperforms GPs, one of the best BO techniques for low-dimensional problems. Even though our method trails slightly behind Bayesian Dropout, these results still illustrate the uncertainty estimation power of our method, even on low-dimensional problems.

## 5.3 CLASSIFICATION

We also look at the problem of image classification. Along with our method having high accuracy, we are also interested in how well our method (and the baseline methods) is able to capture epistemic uncertainty. To measure this we split the dataset into two sets based on class labels: the in distribution dataset and the out of distribution dataset. During training, we only provide the labels for

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the in-distribution dataset. During testing, along with recording the accuracy on the in distribution dataset, we also sample our models on the out of distribution dataset. For such out of distribution examples, we expect the models to show high epistemic uncertainty and, thus, the samples for these out of distribution examples should have high variance.

Note that we still provide the full set of unlabeled training data to our agent. This obviously favors our method over the baseline methods as our method is the only method that is able to take advantage of this unlabeled data. However, from a practical perspective this is a reasonable setup as many realworld datasets have far more unlabeled data than labeled data.

One desired property of an epistemic uncertainty estimator is in constructing confidence sets. For the classification setting, the confidence interval (CI) we consider is the set of possible class predictions. We would expect that this confidence interval contains the true class with high probability. We also expect that, on in-distribution examples, the confidence interval _only_ contains the true label with high probability. To construct these confidence intervals, we take 100 samples from each model on every data point in the test data. We define the metrics CI-Any as the proportion of data points for which _at least one_ sample contains the correct label and CI-All as the proportion of data points for which _every_ sample predicts the correct label.

Another desired property of an epistemic uncertainty estimator is in predicting when a data point is outside the training distribution and, thus, any classification prediction made on such a data point will likely be incorrect. For out of distribution data points, we expect high sample variance from the posterior model and low sample variance from in distribution data points. Thus, variance can be used as a metric to predict which test data points are outside the training distribution. To quantify each method’s ability to perform out of distribution detection using sample variance, we construct ROC curves for each method and measure the area under the curve (AUC).

We tested all methods on both the MNIST dataset and the CIFAR-10 dataset. For MNIST, the in distribtuion classes were [0 _,_ 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 _,_ 4 _,_ 5] and the out of distribution classes were [6 _,_ 7 _,_ 8 _,_ 9]. For CIFAR-10, the in distribtuion classes were [“airplane”, “automobile”, “bird”, “deer”, “dog”, “ship”] and the out-of-distribution classes were [“cat”, “frog”, “horse”, “truck”]. To make sure that each method had a large enough sample variance, we chose hyper-parameters such that prior distributions had near 100% CI-All. To choose the best regularization hyper-parameter, as in the BO experiments, we ran each method with 5 different regularization parameters, then chose the model with both a high validation accuracy and CI-All.

For all other methods, we take 100 samples for measuring CI-Any, CI-All, and sample variance.

Table 1 summarizes the performance of each model using the metrics of test accuracy, CI-Any, CI-All, and the ROC AUC for out of distribution detection. Full ROC curves for each method are plotted in Figure 4. While all methods are able to achieve high prediction accuracy and high _CI-All_ on _in distribution_ examples, our method is the only one to achieve high _CI-Any_ on _out of distribution_ examples, illustrating our method’s ability to construct valid confidence intervals. Additionally, our method significantly out performs all other methods on out of distribution detection.

Figure 5 shows two informative test images, one from the in distribution dataset and one from the out of distribution dataset, for both MNIST and CIFAR-10, along with box plots of sampled PMFs from each of the learned models. While all methods predict the correct label with high precision on the in distribution example, ours has a uniquely wide prediction distribution on the out of distribution example, illustrating high predicted epistemic uncertainty.

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this paper we introduce Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a method to learn a generative model of the Bayesian posterior distribution by regularizing the outputs of the network towards samples of the prior. We prove that under mild assumptions, GPNs approximate samples from the true posterior. We then show empirically that our method is not only competitive with the best uncertainty predictions techniques on small Bayesian Optimization problems, it significantly outperforms these competing methods on high-dimensional classification tasks.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper introduces Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs) as a computationally efficient alternative to traditional ensemble-based methods for epistemic uncertainty estimation, addressing a well-motivated problem in deep learning. The introduction is structurally sound and effectively situates the contribution within the existing literature, particularly by highlighting the prohibitive computational cost of training independent neural network ensembles. However, for a manuscript focused on Bayesian inference, the methodology—specifically how a generative model effectively captures the posterior distribution through regularization towards a prior—remains opaque at this stage. To improve the paper, the authors must move beyond high-level conceptual framing and provide a more rigorous explanation of the training objective and the specific nature of the "low-dimensional latent representation" mentioned in the abstract. Furthermore, the reliance on claims of theoretical alignment with the Bayesian posterior suggests that the subsequent sections must provide clear, explicit proofs that articulate exactly how the GPN framework avoids the pitfalls of variational inference or BNNs while maintaining meaningful uncertainty bounds.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a compelling motivation for Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs) as a scalable alternative to neural network ensembles for epistemic uncertainty estimation, effectively highlighting the computational bottleneck of training independent ensemble members for each posterior sample. The writing is clear and the problem formulation is accessible, appealing to the community's interest in efficient Bayesian deep learning. However, several significant issues must be addressed. First, the novelty of GPNs is questionable; the proposed mechanism of regularizing a network toward prior samples closely resembles established approaches for Bayesian approximation and latent variable posteriors, and the authors must provide a precise technical distinction from this related literature to demonstrate sufficient contribution. Second, the strong theoretical claim that the method approximates the Bayesian posterior requires rigorous verification, as such proofs often rely on restrictive assumptions regarding the prior, likelihood, or network architecture that may limit practical validity; the authors should clarify the scope and limitations of this result. Finally, single-network posterior approximations are historically prone to underestimating uncertainty or suffering from mode collapse compared to ensembles; the empirical evaluation must rigorously demonstrate that GPNs match the reliability and coverage of ensemble baselines, particularly in high-dimensional settings, as any degradation in uncertainty quality would undermine the method's utility despite its sampling efficiency.

comments.explanation: The text presents the Gaussianity of the likelihood in $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}$-space as a direct consequence of Lemma 1 and additive noise, which is mathematically insufficient. While the authors later acknowledge this limitation in Section 4.1 ('this approximation becomes exact when either f is linear or the final layer is infinitely wide'), the immediate derivation glosses over the required network expressiveness assumptions. This gap is significant because Theorem 1 explicitly relies on both the prior and likelihood in output space being Gaussian to derive the posterior via moment matching.

Additionally, the expectation is drawn from $P_{prior}(\theta_{anc})$, whereas the RMS framework defines the anchor distribution as $P_{anc}(\theta_{anc}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{anc}, \Sigma_{anc})$ with a shifted covariance. While the authors later justify using $P_{prior}$ as a practical approximation for tractability, the formal objective in Eq. (13) does not clarify whether $\mu_{anc}$ and $\Sigma_{anc}$ in the second term are fixed hyperparameters or learned quantities. This notation clash and missing specification of the anchor distribution's parameters in the objective could mislead a reader trying to implement the exact training loss.

comments.quote: Then, if we take the limit as M →∞, P_ ( **y** _obs |_ **Y[ˆ]** ) = _P_ ( **y** _obs |_ _**θ**_ ) _._
comments.explanation: I first wondered whether conditioning on the discrete output vector $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}$ can truly become equivalent to conditioning on the full parameter vector $\theta$ as $M \to \infty$. The lemma implicitly assumes that dense sampling of the input space preserves all parameter-level likelihood information. However, for high-dimensional neural networks, the mapping $\theta \mapsto \hat{\mathbf{Y}}$ is many-to-one due to parameter symmetries and over-parameterization. Even in the limit of infinite evaluation points, multiple distinct $\theta$ configurations can produce identical $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}$ vectors, meaning $P(y_{obs}|\hat{\mathbf{Y}})$ discards information present in $P(y_{obs}|\theta)$ that does not affect the output. The lemma does not state that the network must be sufficiently expressive or that we are working in a minimal-parameter regime. While common in this literature, the missing caveat means a careful reader might incorrectly assume the output-space posterior is always fully equivalent to the parameter-space posterior without considering network capacity or identifiability.

comments.title: Misapplied batch index to fixed input grid
comments.explanation: The input grid $\mathbf{x}_{\text{sample}}$ was defined earlier in the paper as a fixed discretization of the input domain $\mathcal{X}$ over $M$ points. The superscript $[j]$ denotes the $j$-th sample in the optimization batch of size $k$ and should only apply to $\mathbf{z}^{[j]}$ and $\theta^{[j]}$. Applying it to $\mathbf{x}_{\text{sample}}$ incorrectly implies the input evaluation grid changes per batch sample, which contradicts the problem setup. The notation should be corrected to $\mathbf{x}_{\text{sample}}$ without the $[j]$ index.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'learning a mapping' vs. optimizing latent codes
comments.quote: To do this, we need to learn a mapping from anchor parameters _**θ**_ anc to embedding vectors **z** . For our experiments, we used a 1-1 embedding scheme where we sample _k_ parameters from the true prior _**θ**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**θ** k ∼ P_ prior( _**θ**_ ) and _k_ independent samples from our embedding **z** 1 _, . . . ,_ **z** _k_ . We then jointly optimize over _**φ**_ and **z** 1 _, . . . ,_ **z** _k_ as follows:
comments.explanation: The text states the goal is to 'learn a mapping' from anchor parameters to embeddings, which conventionally implies training a parametric encoder network (e.g., $h(\theta_{\text{anc}}) \to \mathbf{z}$). However, the procedure describes directly optimizing the embedding vectors $\mathbf{z}^{[j]}$ as trainable parameters for a fixed set of prior samples (an empirical codebook or latent variable optimization approach). While this is a valid and standard technique in generative modeling, the phrasing could mislead readers expecting a learnable neural mapping function. Clarifying that $\mathbf{z}$ is treated as a set of direct optimization variables/codebook entries rather than learning an explicit parametric mapping would improve reproducibility and conceptual clarity.

comments.title: Underspecified mechanism for utilizing unlabeled data
comments.quote: Note that we still provide the full set of unlabeled training data to our agent. This obviously favors our method over the baseline methods as our method is the only method that is able to take advantage of this unlabeled data.
comments.explanation: The authors openly acknowledge that the experimental setup favors their method by providing unlabeled training data that baselines cannot use. However, they fail to specify how GPN actually incorporates this unlabeled data. The theoretical derivation in Section 4.2 and the practical loss function rely only on labeled observations (`y_obs`) and prior samples. Without details on whether the unlabeled data is used for input coverage (`x_sample`), consistency regularization, pseudo-labeling, or another semi-supervised mechanism, the claim remains an unsupported advantage. This omission creates a reproducibility gap and leaves the fairness of the comparison unclear.

comments.title: Contradictory interpretation of CI-Any on out-of-distribution data
comments.quote: While all methods are able to achieve high prediction accuracy and high CI-All on in distribution examples, our method is the only one to achieve high CI-Any on out of distribution examples, illustrating our method’s ability to construct valid confidence intervals.
comments.explanation: CI-Any is defined as the proportion of data points where at least one posterior sample contains the correct label. In the preceding paragraph, the authors state that for out-of-distribution (OOD) examples, 'any classification prediction made on such a data point will likely be incorrect.' A well-calibrated OOD detector should ideally exhibit high uncertainty, often resulting in low confidence across classes or explicit rejection, which would typically yield *low* CI-Any for the true OOD label. Claiming that *high* CI-Any on OOD data demonstrates 'valid confidence intervals' is logically inverted and contradicts their own stated expectation that OOD predictions will be incorrect. This suggests a confusion between coverage metrics on in-distribution data and uncertainty quantification on OOD data.

comments.title: Misapplied terminology regarding CI-All and prior distributions
comments.quote: To make sure that each method had a large enough sample variance, we chose hyper-parameters such that prior distributions had near 100% CI-All.
comments.explanation: The CI-All metric is explicitly defined earlier as the proportion of *test data points* for which every sample predicts the correct label. 'Prior distributions' are theoretical constructs over model parameters or function outputs and do not directly yield a dataset-level metric like CI-All. The authors likely intend to say that the hyperparameters were selected to yield ~100% CI-All on a held-out validation set, or that the prior initialization itself induces high confidence. The current phrasing is technically inaccurate and could mislead readers regarding the hyperparameter tuning procedure and the relationship between prior calibration and posterior variance.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a compelling motivation for Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs) as a scalable alternative to neural network ensembles for epistemic uncertainty estimation, effectively highlighting the computational bottleneck of training independent ensemble members for each posterior sample. The writing is clear and the problem formulation is accessible, appealing to the community's interest in efficient Bayesian deep learning. However, several significant issues must be addressed. First, the novelty of GPNs is questionable; the proposed mechanism of regularizing a network toward prior samples closely resembles established approaches for Bayesian approximation and latent variable posteriors, and the authors must provide a precise technical distinction from this related literature to demonstrate sufficient contribution. Second, the strong theoretical claim that the method approximates the Bayesian posterior requires rigorous verification, as such proofs often rely on restrictive assumptions regarding the prior, likelihood, or network architecture that may limit practical validity; the authors should clarify the scope and limitations of this result. Finally, single-network posterior approximations are historically prone to underestimating uncertainty or suffering from mode collapse compared to ensembles; the empirical evaluation must rigorously demonstrate that GPNs match the reliability and coverage of ensemble baselines, particularly in high-dimensional settings, as any degradation in uncertainty quality would undermine the method's utility despite its sampling efficiency.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency between input/output specification and equations in Section 4.2

The authors likely intended to state that `g` outputs `Y_MAP(Y_anc)` to maintain consistency with the output-space MAP function, or the equations should have been updated to use `Y_anc`. As written, it is unclear whether the generative model operates on parameter-space or output-space anchors, which hinders reproducibility and conceptual clarity.

comments.quote: show empirically that our method can improves uncertainty estimation over competing methods.
comments.explanation: The verb 'improves' is incorrectly conjugated for the modal 'can'. It should read 'can improve'. This is a superficial typo that does not affect the technical content.

comments.title: Missing word in Section 4.3

comments.explanation: The phrase omits a noun such as 'level' or 'amount' between 'same' and 'of'. It currently reads grammatically incomplete ('same of fidelity').

comments.title: Stray asterisk in citation
comments.quote: (Curi* et al., 2020)
comments.explanation: An asterisk appears after the author name 'Curi' in the Related Work section. This is likely a stray footnote marker or OCR artifact and should be removed for standard citation formatting.

comments.title: Incorrect distribution specified for generating output prior samples
comments.quote: Note that samples from the prior of **Y[ˆ]** can be easily obtained by sampling _**θ** ∼ P_ like( _**θ**_ ) and computing _Y_[ˆ] _i_ = _f_ ( _x[i]_ sample[;] _**[ θ]**_)[for][all] _[i]_.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that prior predictive samples for the output space can be generated by sampling parameters from the likelihood $P_{\text{like}}(\theta)$. This is fundamentally incorrect. To draw samples from the prior predictive distribution over outputs, one must sample from the parameter prior $P_{\text{prior}}(\theta)$ and pass them through the network $f$. Sampling from the likelihood does not produce prior samples and lacks a coherent probabilistic interpretation in this context. The authors later correctly state in Section 4.2 that 'we simply sample a set of parameters $\theta \sim P_{\text{prior}}(\theta)$', which directly contradicts the statement in Section 4.1. As written, this instruction is mathematically invalid and would lead to incorrect model initialization or prior estimation if followed literally.

comments.title: Notation conflates random variable with distribution mean in shifted prior

comments.explanation: The notation here uses $\theta_{\text{anc}}$ as both the argument of the probability density function and the mean parameter of the Gaussian. Formally, a PDF should be written as $P(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{anc}}) = \mathcal{N}(\theta; \theta_{\text{anc}}, \Sigma_{\text{prior}})$, indicating that the distribution is over $\theta$ centered at the sampled anchor. While the mathematical intent (centering a Gaussian prior at the anchor point) is standard in RMS and easily understood, the formal notation is inconsistent and could confuse readers tracking the variable dependencies.

comments.explanation: Section 4 explicitly reformulates the problem to operate in *output space* rather than parameter space to avoid the challenges of optimizing over high-dimensional parameter spaces. However, the text states that $g$ outputs $\theta_{\text{MAP}}(\theta_{\text{anc}})$. This contradicts the section's premise and the subsequent loss function, where $g$ is evaluated at $x_{\text{sample}}$ to compare against prior network outputs. It is almost certainly a typographical error for $\hat{Y}_{\text{MAP}}$, but as written, it suggests the GPN predicts parameters, which undermines the stated motivation for the output-space reformulation and obscures the interpretation of the training objective.

comments.quote: It turns out that, since the observation noise is additive, the likelihood in parameter space _P_ ( **y** obs _|_ _**θ**_ ) is also Gaussian as a function of the output vector **Y[ˆ]** . Thus, if we take _|_ **x** sample _| →∞_ and apply Lemma 1, we get that the output likelihood _P_ ( **y** obs _|_ **Y[ˆ]** ) is Gaussian as a function of **Y[ˆ]** .
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the claim that additive noise directly implies the likelihood is Gaussian with respect to the output vector $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}$. I checked the setup: $P(y_{obs}|\theta)$ is indeed Gaussian in $\theta$ (under the paper's Gaussian parameter-likelihood assumption), but $\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = f(x_{sample}; \theta)$ is a non-linear transformation of $\theta$. A non-linear pushforward of a Gaussian distribution is generally non-Gaussian. The text presents this Gaussianity in $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}$-space as a direct consequence of Lemma 1 and additive noise, which is mathematically insufficient. While the authors later acknowledge this limitation in Section 4.1 ('this approximation becomes exact when either f is linear or the final layer is infinitely wide'), the immediate derivation glosses over the required network expressiveness assumptions. This gap is significant because Theorem 1 explicitly relies on both the prior and likelihood in output space being Gaussian to derive the posterior via moment matching.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency and undefined subscript in Equation (13)

This notation clash and missing specification of the anchor distribution's parameters in the objective could mislead a reader trying to implement the exact training loss.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper introduces Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a method for epistemic uncertainty estimation that aims to address the computational limitations of ensemble-based approaches by learning a generative model that directly approximates the posterior distribution over functions. The paper has several strengths: the problem motivation is compelling and clearly articulated—ensembles are powerful but computationally prohibitive for repeated sampling, and the proposed solution of learning a latent representation that enables fast posterior sampling is conceptually elegant. However, the manuscript suffers from several significant issues that must be addressed before it can be considered for publication. Most critically, the authors claim theoretical proof that GPNs "approximate the Bayesian posterior" but provide no sketch of this proof or even a theorem statement in the introduction or related work, which makes the claim feel unsubstantiated and undermines the paper's central contribution. The presentation is also quite vague about the actual mechanism: the description "regularizing the network towards samples from the prior" is insufficient to understand what loss function or training procedure is actually used, and the single figure showing 2D embedding samples provides little insight into how the method works or why it should succeed where other approaches fail. Additionally, the paper lacks quantitative results entirely within the provided excerpt—no metrics, baselines, or experimental setup are described—making it impossible to evaluate the empirical claim of improvement over competing methods. The clarity is further hampered by a somewhat disorganized flow that introduces the problem, discusses related work at length, but never concretely explains the proposed method before the excerpt ends. The writing quality is acceptable but would benefit from more precise technical language and fewer informal phrases. Overall, the idea is promising, but the paper requires substantially more technical depth—both theoretical and methodological—to be convincing.

comments.title: Grammatical error in introduction
comments.quote: We prove that our method approximates the Bayesian posterior over functions and show empirically that our method can improves uncertainty estimation over competing methods.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'can improves' is grammatically incorrect; it should be 'can improve' or simply 'improves'. This appears to be a typo that would confuse readers if left in the final version.

comments.title: OCR-induced garbled phrase in related work
comments.quote: Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) (Blundell et al., 2015) are another way to perform Bayesian Inference a neural networks by modeling each parameter in the network with an independent Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'Bayesian Inference a neural networks' is garbled, likely due to OCR misreading. The intended text is almost certainly 'Bayesian Inference in neural networks' or 'Bayesian Inference for neural networks'. The stray 'a' disrupts reading and is not a valid mathematical statement.

comments.title: Incorrect distribution for sampling prior of Ŷ
comments.quote: Note that samples from the prior of **Y[ˆ]** can be easily obtained by sampling _**θ** ∼ P_ like( _**θ**_ ) and computing _Y_[ˆ] _i_ = _f_ ( _x[i]_ sample[;] _**[ θ]**_[)][for][all] _[i]
comments.explanation: The prior of Ŷ should be induced by the prior over parameters θ, not by the likelihood. The text says 'sampling θ ∼ P_like(θ)', but P_like is the likelihood (conditioned on data) and is not a distribution over θ. This is likely a typographical error (perhaps 'P_prior' was intended). The error could mislead readers about how to generate prior samples for the anchor distribution. Context does not resolve it because P_like appears correctly elsewhere as a function of y_obs given θ.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for shifted prior distribution in RMS
comments.quote: Each anchor point defines a shifted prior distribution _P_ anc( _**θ**_ anc) = _N_ ( _**θ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** prior).
comments.explanation: The notation P_anc(θ_anc) suggests the distribution of the anchor point itself, but the intended meaning is that the shifted prior over parameters θ is centered at θ_anc, i.e., P_anc(θ) = N(θ; θ_anc, Σ_prior). The text later correctly uses log P_anc(θ) in Equation (8), which expects a function of θ, not of θ_anc. This inconsistency could confuse readers about the role of the anchor.

comments.title: Potential missing square in likelihood variance
comments.explanation: The Gaussian distribution is typically parameterized with variance, not standard deviation. The notation N(μ, σ) is ambiguous; here the second argument appears to be the standard deviation σ_ε, implying variance σ_ε^2. Later in the paper (e.g., in derived equations) σ_ε^2 is used, creating an inconsistency. While many papers use this shorthand, the inconsistency with later usage could cause confusion when readers apply standard formulas.

comments.title: Σ_like used in Theorem 1 without definition in output space
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 states that if we choose these values for μ_anc and Σ_anc, then the distribution of MAP solutions equals the posterior in output space. However, Σ_like is not defined in the output space. In the parameter space, Σ_like was introduced as the covariance of the parameter likelihood. For the output-space formulation, the likelihood P(y_obs|Ŷ) is implicitly Gaussian, but its covariance matrix (which would be Σ_like in the output space) is not explicitly defined. The theorem's statement is therefore incomplete without clarifying what Σ_like refers to in this context.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of network input and output in practical implementation
comments.quote: Now, if we want to learn the MAP function in the output space, we can construct a neural network g , parameterized by some vector φ , that takes a sample from the output anchor distribution, Ŷ_anc ∼ N (μ anc, Σ anc), along with the evaluation points x sample, and outputs θ MAP(θ anc).
comments.explanation: The text first says g inputs an output-space anchor Ŷ_anc but then states it outputs θ_MAP(θ_anc), which is a parameter-space MAP.  This is internally contradictory.  Moreover, the objective in equation (13) immediately after uses expectation over the parameter-space anchor θ_anc (drawn from the prior) and writes the network as g(x_sample, θ_anc; φ), not Ŷ_anc.  The derivation of the loss (the equation following (13)) also uses δ_j = g(x_sample^j, θ_anc; φ) - f(x_sample^j; θ_anc), again treating the input as θ_anc.  The reader cannot determine whether the network actually operates in output space (taking Ŷ_anc as input) or in parameter space (taking θ_anc as input).  This discrepancy undermines the paper's claim that GPNs apply Randomized MAP Sampling in output space and makes the practical algorithm unclear.

comments.title: Unclear discretization condition in Lemma 1
comments.quote: Let x_sample be some discretization over the input space of M[d] points such l that for any x ∈ X there exists an x′ ∈ x_sample where ‖x − x′‖_2 ≤ M[for some constant][l].
comments.explanation: The notation is garbled (likely OCR artefacts: 'M[d]' probably intended as 'M^d', 'such l' as 'such that'), but the stated bound ‖x − x′‖_2 ≤ M is problematic because M is the number of points along a dimension and goes to infinity, whereas a denseness condition should involve an inverse power of M (e.g., ≤ C / M^{1/d}).  As written, the bound does not force the discretization to become dense; instead it becomes looser as M grows.  Since Lemma 1 relies on the limit M → ∞ to equate P(y_obs | Ŷ) and P(y_obs | θ), the condition must ensure that the observed inputs are covered arbitrarily well.  The intended meaning is probably clear to the authors, but the presented statement is too ambiguous to be evaluated without consulting the appendix.

comments.title: Imprecise claim about Gaussianity of likelihood as function of Ŷ
comments.quote: It turns out that, since the observation noise is additive, the likelihood in parameter space P(y_obs|θ) is also Gaussian as a function of the output vector Ŷ.
comments.explanation: Strictly speaking, P(y_obs|θ) is a function of θ, not of Ŷ.  The intended meaning is that when the data-likelihood is re-expressed through the deterministic mapping Ŷ = f(x_sample; θ), it can be written as P(y_obs|Ŷ) = Π N(y_obs^i | Ŷ_{j(i)}, σ_ε), which is Gaussian in Ŷ.  The phrasing as written could mislead a reader into thinking the parameter-space density itself depends on Ŷ in a functional way.  This is a minor clarity issue that is resolved by reading the surrounding context, but it is worth noting.

comments.title: Inconsistent input convention for network g in embedding section
comments.quote: δj = g( x_sample[j] , z_j + ε; φ) − f( x_sample[j]; θ_j )
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper (e.g., Eq. 13–14, summary), the network g is defined as taking the full set of sample points x_sample and an anchor parameter θ_anc, returning a vector of function values of length |x_sample|. In the low-dimensional embedding passage, the same symbol g is used with a single input point x_sample[j] as its first argument, implying pointwise evaluation. While the network could be queried at individual points, the paper does not clarify this shift in convention, and the training objective (not shown in the passage) would need to aggregate these pointwise outputs into a vector for the likelihood and regularization terms. This inconsistency could confuse a reader trying to reconcile the embedding method with the earlier theoretical development.

comments.title: Ambiguous regularizer notation for embedding distribution
comments.quote: L_reg(z_1, …, z_k) = D_KL( N( z_bar, s_z ), N(0, 1) )
comments.explanation: The KL divergence is written between a multivariate Gaussian (with empirical mean z_bar and covariance/scale s_z) and a univariate standard normal N(0,1). The dimensions are mismatched: if z is a d‑dimensional embedding, the second argument should be a multivariate normal N(0, I_d) or a product of univariate KL terms. The paper does not specify whether s_z is a covariance matrix, a diagonal covariance, or a scalar variance, nor whether the KL is computed per dimension or jointly. This underspecification hinders reproducibility of the regularizer.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of embedding mapping and initialization
comments.quote: To do this, we need to learn a mapping from anchor parameters θ_anc to embedding vectors z. ... k independent samples from our embedding z_1, …, z_k.
comments.explanation: The method described does not learn an explicit mapping; instead, it optimizes a separate embedding vector z_i for each sampled anchor parameter (a 1-1 embedding scheme). No function from θ_anc to z is ever constructed or used at inference time. Additionally, the phrase 'samples from our embedding' is ambiguous: the z_i are trainable parameters, not fixed samples. The paper should clarify that the z_i are initialized from N(0,I) and then optimized, and that no mapping is learned.

comments.title: Classification application violates Gaussian assumptions
comments.quote: We also look at the problem of image classification. … We tested all methods on both the MNIST dataset and the CIFAR-10 dataset.
comments.explanation: The paper's theoretical foundation (Theorem 1, Section 4.1) relies on a Gaussian output prior and a Gaussian likelihood to prove that GPNs approximate the Bayesian posterior. For image classification with a softmax likelihood, these assumptions are not satisfied. The authors do not describe any modification to the GPN framework (e.g., using a Gaussian on logits or a different objective) to accommodate classification, nor do they justify why the theoretical guarantees would still hold. Without such justification, the experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 lack a solid theoretical basis, making the claim that GPNs approximate the posterior in classification tasks unsupported.

comments.title: Unlabeled data gives unfair advantage without methodological justification
comments.quote: Note that we still provide the full set of unlabeled training data to our agent. This obviously favors our method over the baseline methods as our method is the only method that is able to take advantage of this unlabeled data.
comments.explanation: The GPN description in earlier sections (e.g., Equation 13 and 14) only uses labeled data (y_obs) and prior samples (θ_anc) to learn the MAP function. There is no mechanism described that incorporates unlabeled data beyond possibly defining the input grid x_sample. Yet in the classification experiments, unlabeled data is provided exclusively to GPN, which the authors admit favors their method. The baselines (GP, Bayesian Dropout, Ensemble) could potentially be adapted to use unlabeled data (e.g., via semi-supervised learning), but they are not given that opportunity. This makes the comparison unfair and the reported improvements potentially attributable to the extra data rather than the GPN algorithm itself. The passage does not explain how the unlabeled data is used within the GPN framework, leaving the methodology underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing 'prior distributions had near 100% CI-All'
comments.quote: To make sure that each method had a large enough sample variance, we chose hyper-parameters such that prior distributions had near 100% CI-All.
comments.explanation: The term 'prior distributions' is ambiguous here. In context, the authors are describing hyperparameter tuning for all methods. It likely refers to the predictive distributions (i.e., samples from the posterior) on the validation set, not the actual prior over functions. However, a reader could misinterpret it as requiring the prior itself to have near-perfect confidence, which is nonsensical. This ambiguity could momentarily confuse readers, though the intended meaning can be inferred from the surrounding discussion about sample variance and CI-All metrics.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper introduces Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a method for epistemic uncertainty estimation that aims to address the computational limitations of ensemble-based approaches by learning a generative model that directly approximates the posterior distribution over functions. The paper has several strengths: the problem motivation is compelling and clearly articulated—ensembles are powerful but computationally prohibitive for repeated sampling, and the proposed solution of learning a latent representation that enables fast posterior sampling is conceptually elegant. However, the manuscript suffers from several significant issues that must be addressed before it can be considered for publication. Most critically, the authors claim theoretical proof that GPNs "approximate the Bayesian posterior" but provide no sketch of this proof or even a theorem statement in the introduction or related work, which makes the claim feel unsubstantiated and undermines the paper's central contribution. The presentation is also quite vague about the actual mechanism: the description "regularizing the network towards samples from the prior" is insufficient to understand what loss function or training procedure is actually used, and the single figure showing 2D embedding samples provides little insight into how the method works or why it should succeed where other approaches fail. Additionally, the paper lacks quantitative results entirely within the provided excerpt—no metrics, baselines, or experimental setup are described—making it impossible to evaluate the empirical claim of improvement over competing methods. The clarity is further hampered by a somewhat disorganized flow that introduces the problem, discusses related work at length, but never concretely explains the proposed method before the excerpt ends. The writing quality is acceptable but would benefit from more precise technical language and fewer informal phrases. Overall, the idea is promising, but the paper requires substantially more technical depth—both theoretical and methodological—to be convincing.

comments.title: Grammatical error in introduction
comments.quote: We prove that our method approximates the Bayesian posterior over functions and show empirically that our method can improves uncertainty estimation over competing methods.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'can improves' is grammatically incorrect; it should be 'can improve' or simply 'improves'. This appears to be a typo that would confuse readers if left in the final version.

comments.title: OCR-induced garbled phrase in related work
comments.quote: Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) (Blundell et al., 2015) are another way to perform Bayesian Inference a neural networks by modeling each parameter in the network with an independent Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'Bayesian Inference a neural networks' is garbled, likely due to OCR misreading. The intended text is almost certainly 'Bayesian Inference in neural networks' or 'Bayesian Inference for neural networks'. The stray 'a' disrupts reading and is not a valid mathematical statement.

comments.title: Incorrect distribution for sampling prior of Ŷ
comments.quote: Note that samples from the prior of **Y[ˆ]** can be easily obtained by sampling _**θ** ∼ P_ like( _**θ**_ ) and computing _Y_[ˆ] _i_ = _f_ ( _x[i]_ sample[;] _**[ θ]**_[)][for][all] _[i]
comments.explanation: The prior of Ŷ should be induced by the prior over parameters θ, not by the likelihood. The text says 'sampling θ ∼ P_like(θ)', but P_like is the likelihood (conditioned on data) and is not a distribution over θ. This is likely a typographical error (perhaps 'P_prior' was intended). The error could mislead readers about how to generate prior samples for the anchor distribution. Context does not resolve it because P_like appears correctly elsewhere as a function of y_obs given θ.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for shifted prior distribution in RMS
comments.quote: Each anchor point defines a shifted prior distribution _P_ anc( _**θ**_ anc) = _N_ ( _**θ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** prior).
comments.explanation: The notation P_anc(θ_anc) suggests the distribution of the anchor point itself, but the intended meaning is that the shifted prior over parameters θ is centered at θ_anc, i.e., P_anc(θ) = N(θ; θ_anc, Σ_prior). The text later correctly uses log P_anc(θ) in Equation (8), which expects a function of θ, not of θ_anc. This inconsistency could confuse readers about the role of the anchor.

comments.title: Potential missing square in likelihood variance
comments.explanation: The Gaussian distribution is typically parameterized with variance, not standard deviation. The notation N(μ, σ) is ambiguous; here the second argument appears to be the standard deviation σ_ϵ, implying variance σ_ϵ^2. Later in the paper (e.g., in derived equations) σ_ϵ^2 is used, creating an inconsistency. While many papers use this shorthand, the inconsistency with later usage could cause confusion when readers apply standard formulas.

comments.title: Σ_like used in Theorem 1 without definition in output space
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 states that if we choose these values for µ_anc and Σ_anc, then the distribution of MAP solutions equals the posterior in output space. However, Σ_like is not defined in the output space. In the parameter space, Σ_like was introduced as the covariance of the parameter likelihood. For the output-space formulation, the likelihood P(y_obs|Ŷ) is implicitly Gaussian, but its covariance matrix (which would be Σ_like in the output space) is not explicitly defined. The theorem's statement is therefore incomplete without clarifying what Σ_like refers to in this context.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of network input and output in practical implementation
comments.quote: Now, if we want to learn the MAP function in the output space, we can construct a neural network g , parameterized by some vector φ , that takes a sample from the output anchor distribution, Ŷ_anc ∼ N (μ anc, Σ anc), along with the evaluation points x sample, and outputs θ MAP(θ anc).
comments.explanation: The text first says g inputs an output-space anchor Ŷ_anc but then states it outputs θ_MAP(θ_anc), which is a parameter-space MAP.  This is internally contradictory.  Moreover, the objective in equation (13) immediately after uses expectation over the parameter-space anchor θ_anc (drawn from the prior) and writes the network as g(x_sample, θ_anc; φ), not Ŷ_anc.  The derivation of the loss (the equation following (13)) also uses δ_j = g(x_sample^j, θ_anc; φ) - f(x_sample^j; θ_anc), again treating the input as θ_anc.  The reader cannot determine whether the network actually operates in output space (taking Ŷ_anc as input) or in parameter space (taking θ_anc as input).  This discrepancy undermines the paper's claim that GPNs apply Randomized MAP Sampling in output space and makes the practical algorithm unclear.

comments.title: Unclear discretization condition in Lemma 1
comments.quote: Let x_sample be some discretization over the input space of M[d] points such l that for any x ∈ X there exists an x′ ∈ x_sample where ‖x − x′‖_2 ≤ M[for some constant][l].
comments.explanation: The notation is garbled (likely OCR artefacts: 'M[d]' probably intended as 'M^d', 'such l' as 'such that'), but the stated bound ‖x − x′‖_2 ≤ M is problematic because M is the number of points along a dimension and goes to infinity, whereas a denseness condition should involve an inverse power of M (e.g., ≤ C / M^{1/d}).  As written, the bound does not force the discretization to become dense; instead it becomes looser as M grows.  Since Lemma 1 relies on the limit M → ∞ to equate P(y_obs | Ŷ) and P(y_obs | θ), the condition must ensure that the observed inputs are covered arbitrarily well.  The intended meaning is probably clear to the authors, but the presented statement is too ambiguous to be evaluated without consulting the appendix.

comments.title: Undefined Σ_like in output-space Theorem 1
comments.quote: If we choose the distribution over Ŷ_anc to be P(Ŷ_anc) = N(μ_anc, Σ_anc), where μ_anc = μ_Ŷ and Σ_anc = Σ_Ŷ + Σ_Ŷ Σ_like^{-1} Σ_Ŷ
comments.explanation: Σ_like was defined earlier (equation (4)) as the covariance of the parameter-likelihood P_param-like(θ; y_obs) for the parameter vector θ.  In the output-space context, the analogous quantity would be the covariance of the likelihood P(y_obs | Ŷ) viewed as a function of Ŷ, but this has not been introduced or denoted.  The reader must guess that the same symbol Σ_like now refers to a different object, which creates ambiguity.  Because the proof is relegated to the appendix, this might be clarified there, but in the main text it is a missing definition that could hinder understanding.

comments.title: Imprecise claim about Gaussianity of likelihood as function of Ŷ
comments.quote: It turns out that, since the observation noise is additive, the likelihood in parameter space P(y_obs|θ) is also Gaussian as a function of the output vector Ŷ.
comments.explanation: Strictly speaking, P(y_obs|θ) is a function of θ, not of Ŷ.  The intended meaning is that when the data-likelihood is re-expressed through the deterministic mapping Ŷ = f(x_sample; θ), it can be written as P(y_obs|Ŷ) = ∏ N(y_obs^i | Ŷ_{j(i)}, σ_ϵ), which is Gaussian in Ŷ.  The phrasing as written could mislead a reader into thinking the parameter-space density itself depends on Ŷ in a functional way.  This is a minor clarity issue that is resolved by reading the surrounding context, but it is worth noting.

comments.title: Inconsistent input convention for network g in embedding section
comments.quote: δj = g( x_sample[j] , z_j + ε; φ) − f( x_sample[j]; θ_j )
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper (e.g., Eq. 13–14, summary), the network g is defined as taking the full set of sample points x_sample and an anchor parameter θ_anc, returning a vector of function values of length |x_sample|. In the low-dimensional embedding passage, the same symbol g is used with a single input point x_sample[j] as its first argument, implying pointwise evaluation. While the network could be queried at individual points, the paper does not clarify this shift in convention, and the training objective (not shown in the passage) would need to aggregate these pointwise outputs into a vector for the likelihood and regularization terms. This inconsistency could confuse a reader trying to reconcile the embedding method with the earlier theoretical development.

comments.title: Ambiguous regularizer notation for embedding distribution
comments.quote: L_reg(z_1, …, z_k) = D_KL( N( z_bar, s_z ), N(0, 1) )
comments.explanation: The KL divergence is written between a multivariate Gaussian (with empirical mean z_bar and covariance/scale s_z) and a univariate standard normal N(0,1). The dimensions are mismatched: if z is a d‑dimensional embedding, the second argument should be a multivariate normal N(0, I_d) or a product of univariate KL terms. The paper does not specify whether s_z is a covariance matrix, a diagonal covariance, or a scalar variance, nor whether the KL is computed per dimension or jointly. This underspecification hinders reproducibility of the regularizer.

comments.title: Misleading phrasing about learning a mapping to embedding vectors
comments.quote: To do this, we need to learn a mapping from anchor parameters θ_anc to embedding vectors z.
comments.explanation: The method described immediately after does not learn an explicit mapping; instead, it optimizes a separate embedding vector z_i for each sampled anchor parameter θ_i (a 1‑1 embedding scheme). No function from θ_anc to z is ever constructed or used at inference time – during sampling, new z vectors are drawn from N(0,I). The phrase 'learn a mapping' suggests a learned transformation, which is not what the implementation does. This could mislead readers about the actual procedure.

comments.title: Ambiguous initialization of embedding vectors
comments.quote: k independent samples from our embedding z_1, …, z_k.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'samples from our embedding' is ambiguous: it could mean the z_i are drawn from N(0,I) and then fixed, but the subsequent sentence says they are jointly optimized over φ and z_1,…,z_k, implying the z_i are trainable parameters. The word 'samples' is inconsistent with the optimization treatment. The paper should clarify that the z_i are initialized from N(0,I) and then optimized, not sampled once and kept fixed.

comments.title: Classification application violates Gaussian assumptions
comments.quote: We also look at the problem of image classification. … We tested all methods on both the MNIST dataset and the CIFAR-10 dataset.
comments.explanation: The paper's theoretical foundation (Theorem 1, Section 4.1) relies on a Gaussian output prior and a Gaussian likelihood to prove that GPNs approximate the Bayesian posterior. For image classification with a softmax likelihood, these assumptions are not satisfied. The authors do not describe any modification to the GPN framework (e.g., using a Gaussian on logits or a different objective) to accommodate classification, nor do they justify why the theoretical guarantees would still hold. Without such justification, the experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 lack a solid theoretical basis, making the claim that GPNs approximate the posterior in classification tasks unsupported.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper addresses the significant computational bottleneck of training large ensembles for epistemic uncertainty quantification, a well-motivated challenge in safety-critical domains like autonomous manufacturing. The manuscript is clearly written, offering a persuasive argument for GPNs as a theoretically grounded yet computationally efficient alternative to resampling methods. However, the central theoretical contribution—that the GPN architecture approximates the Bayesian posterior—is ambitious and will require rigorous justification in the detailed methodology section. Specifically, the reviewer will need to scrutinize whether the low-dimensional latent representation effectively captures the full complexity of the high-dimensional posterior distribution, as a focus on a simplified latent space risks introducing biases or approximations that compromise the utility of the uncertainty estimates compared to full ensembles.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation and sampling in reformulation to output space
comments.quote: $\phi = \arg\max E_{\theta_{anc} \sim P_{prior}(\theta_{anc})} \log P_\theta(y_{obs} | g(x_{sample}, \theta_{anc}; \phi))$
comments.explanation:
The paper presents a reformulation from the parameter space to the output space (anchor distribution), but this section contains multiple interconnected technical errors:

1.  **Naming Contradiction:** The author uses the notation P_{like}(\theta) to describe the prior distribution over \boldsymbol{\hat{Y}}. This contradicts the explicit definition of P_{like} as the likelihood function and creates a direct inconsistency with the problem statement.
2.  **Notation Ambiguity:** The definition of the shifted prior P_{anc}(\theta_{anc}) uses the random sample \theta_{anc} as the argument of the distribution function, conflating the random variable with the general parameter \theta.
3.  **Sampling Logic Error:** The optimization objective samples \theta_{anc} from the parameter prior P_{prior}(\theta_{anc}) rather than from the output anchor distribution P_{anc}(\hat{Y}_{anc}) as the text describes. In the reformulated framework, sampling should occur in the output space, not by directly sampling the parameter prior in the objective function.

The manuscript is clearly written, offering a persuasive argument for GPNs as a theoretically grounded yet computationally efficient alternative to resampling methods. However, the central theoretical contribution—that the GPN architecture approximates the Bayesian posterior—is ambitious and will require rigorous justification in the detailed methodology section. Specifically, the reviewer will need to scrutinize whether the low-dimensional latent representation effectively captures the full complexity of the high-dimensional posterior distribution, as a focus on a simplified latent space risks introducing biases or approximations that compromise the utility of the uncertainty estimates compared to full ensembles.

comments.title: Incorrect notation for Prior distribution in Section 4.1
comments.quote: Note that samples from the prior of \boldsymbol{\hat{Y}} can be easily obtained by sampling \theta \sim P_{like}(\theta)
comments.explanation: In the preceding paragraph, the author defines the prior distribution over parameters as P_{prior}(\theta). However, when discussing the prior distribution over the transformed random variable \boldsymbol{\hat{Y}}, the author incorrectly cites P_{like}(\theta). In Equation (1), P_{like} is explicitly defined as the likelihood of the observed data given the parameters. Using P_{like} to denote the prior distribution creates a direct contradiction with the definitions established in the Problem Statement and violates consistency in notation. A careful reader would be confused by this terminology.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for shifted prior distribution
comments.quote: Each anchor point defines a shifted prior distribution P_{anc}(\theta_{anc}) = N(\theta_{anc} , \Sigma_{prior})
comments.explanation: The text uses \theta_{anc} to denote a random sample from the anchor distribution. It then defines a 'shifted prior' distribution P_{anc} such that its mean is \theta_{anc}. However, the notation P_{anc}(\theta_{anc}) suggests the argument of the distribution is the anchor point itself. Later, in Equation (8), the author uses P_{anc}(\theta) as the log-prior term in the MAP objective. This indicates the author likely intended to define the shifted prior as P_{anc}(\theta) = N(\theta_{anc}, \Sigma_{prior}). The current notation conflates the random variable \theta_{anc} with the general parameter \theta.

comments.title: Inconsistent Sampling Distribution in Optimization Objective
comments.quote: $\phi = \arg\max E_{\theta_{anc} \sim P_{prior}(\theta_{anc})} \log P_\theta(y_{obs} | g(x_{sample}, \theta_{anc}; \phi))$
comments.explanation: The text preceding this equation describes constructing a neural network $g$ that 'takes a sample from the output anchor distribution, $\hat{Y}_{anc} \sim N(\mu_{anc}, \Sigma_{anc})$'. However, the objective function defined here samples $\theta_{anc}$ from the *parameter* prior $P_{prior}(\theta_{anc})$, not from the output anchor distribution $P_{anc}(\hat{Y}_{anc})$ derived in Theorem 1. In the reformulation to output space, the anchor distribution is distinct from the prior. This mismatch contradicts the stated reformulation and the method's reliance on the distribution of $\hat{Y}_{anc}$. Ideally, the expectation should be over $\hat{Y}_{anc} \sim P_{anc}(\hat{Y}_{anc})$ (or a corresponding latent embedding), as implied by the description of the sampling procedure.

comments.title: Grammatically and Logically Confusing Sentence
comments.quote: In practice, of course, we cannot compute the regularization term, $\frac{N}{\sigma_\epsilon^2} \delta^T \Sigma_{\hat{Y}}^{-1} \delta$ as $|x_{sample}| \to \infty$.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'we cannot compute ... as ...' is logically awkward. It suggests an operation defined only in the limit is being performed, or that the act of computing a finite approximation is failing as the limit is approached. In standard mathematical writing, one evaluates the expression (using Monte Carlo integration with finite samples) and notes that the resulting term approaches the limit as the grid size increases. The phrasing implies an undefined state rather than the standard approximation step.
